content: Antibiotic resistance is spreading primarily due to the selective pressure exerted by widespread and often inappropriate antibiotic use in both clinical and agricultural settings. In hospitals, high antibiotic usage selects for resistant strains, while subtherapeutic dosing in agriculture promotes resistance evolution in zoonotic pathogens. Horizontal gene transfer among bacteria facilitates rapid dissemination of resistance determinants across species and environments, compounding the problem. Environmental contamination through pharmaceutical waste and agricultural runoff creates reservoirs of resistance genes, allowing further amplification beyond clinical contexts. Global travel and trade accelerate the movement of resistant bacteria and genes, enabling rapid international dissemination. Compensatory mutations often mitigate fitness costs associated with resistance, allowing resistant strains to persist and compete effectively even in antibiotic-free environments. Insufficient surveillance and inconsistent antibiotic stewardship policies worldwide hinder timely detection and containment of emerging resistance threats. Thus, antibiotic resistance spread results from a complex interplay of biological mechanisms, environmental factors, human practices, and systemic policy failures, necessitating integrated global interventions.
